topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy dropping onto a wooden desk. Professor Albright was leaving. It wasn't a surprise, not exactly, but the finality of it settled over me like a shroud. I stared at the screen, the words blurring into meaningless characters. My stomach tightened, a cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I picked up my pen, but the ink seemed to swim on the page. Every sentence I’d painstakingly crafted for the thesis felt suddenly hollow, unsupported. I began to pace the small confines of my dorm room, the worn carpet muffling the sound of my anxious footsteps. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I kept re-reading the last feedback notes, searching for some hidden clue, some indication that this departure was temporary, that I hadn't misread the entire landscape of my academic future.

[story 2]

"So, he's really going?" I managed to ask, the sound of my own voice startlingly thin in the quiet faculty lounge. My hands were clenched so tightly around my lukewarm coffee mug that my knuckles were white. I watched Professor Albright across the room, his usual composed demeanor slightly frayed at the edges as he spoke with a colleague. A wave of profound unease washed over me, making the air feel thick and hard to draw into my lungs. I avoided eye contact, focusing instead on the pattern of the chipped paint on the windowsill. My thoughts cycled rapidly—revising chapters, anticipating the administrative chaos, wondering how I was supposed to navigate this sudden void in my guidance. I slumped into the nearest armchair, the fabric rough against my skin, and let my head drop forward until my chin rested on my chest, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

I kept catching myself staring at the door, half-expecting Professor Albright to walk in and shatter the news with a casual shrug. Instead, the reality of his exit hung in the air, heavy and suffocating. My shoulders drooped visibly, pulling my collar up around my neck as if trying to physically shrink away from the attention. I started meticulously cleaning my laptop, an obsessive ritual designed to distract my hands from the churning sensation in my gut. Each swipe of the cloth felt pointless. The weight of the unwritten chapters pressed down on my chest, making every breath shallow. I reread the abstract one more time, the words mocking my effort. It felt like the foundation of my entire project had just been pulled out from under me, leaving me standing on shifting sand.
